takeaway: The four C.R.A.P. principles: Contrast means "avoid elements that are weakly similar by making them strongly different"; Repetition unifies a design through repeated color and fonts; Alignment places elements intentionally; Proximity groups related items by closeness.

This open textbook attributes the C.R.A.P. framework directly to Robin Williams' The Non-Designer's Design Book, calling it "an essential reference used even in graphic design programs" and the principles applicable across all visual media.

Visual hierarchy arranges elements so people instantly recognize their order of importance; tools include size ("Larger elements command more attention than smaller ones"), color, contrast, alignment, repetition, proximity, and white space.

"Color theory is the study of how colors work together and how they affect our emotions and perceptions"; the color wheel (invented by Newton in 1666) organizes hues into primary, secondary, and tertiary categories.
Color harmony is "the balanced and aesthetically pleasing interaction of colors," achieved through schemes like complementary pairs (opposite on the wheel) and analogous sets (adjacent on the wheel).

Color theory is "a historical body of knowledge describing the behavior of colors," including color mixing, contrast, harmony, schemes, and symbolism; "Colors seen together to produce a pleasing affective response are said to be in harmony."
WCAG 2 Level AA requires "a contrast ratio of at least 4.5:1" for normal text and "at least 3:1" for large text; Level AAA demands 7:1 and 4.5:1 respectively.

Do not rely on color alone: "Color is not used as the only visual means of conveying information, indicating an action, prompting a response, or distinguishing a visual element" — supplement with text, icons, or patterns.
Grids are frameworks of columns, gutters, and margins; per NN/g, "grids improve page readability and scannability and allow people to quickly get where they need to go."

The U.S. Web Design System uses a mobile-first 12-column grid: "The grid system uses a series of containers, rows, and columns to lay out and align content," with responsive breakpoints that stack columns on narrow screens.

Page layout is "the arrangement of visual elements on a page"; a grid is "a set of guidelines... for aligning and repeating elements on a page," visible in the design process but invisible to the end user.
"A spatial system is a set of rules for how you measure, size, and space your UI elements," creating visual rhythm and reducing the number of ad hoc spacing decisions.
Per NN/g, whitespace is "an effective principle to achieve a balanced design, making it easier for your users to scan and read"; pair it with a consistent spacing system and the proximity principle.

Butterick's core body-text rules: point size "10–12 point" in print or "15–25 pixels" on web; line spacing "120–145%" of point size; line length "45–90 characters per line"; and choose a professional font over system defaults.
Butterick names the four body-text levers — point size, line spacing, line length, font choice — and calls a professional font "the easiest and most visible improvement" you can make to typography.

Typography is "the discipline designers use to arrange typefaces in a user interface to ensure text is legible, readable, and scalable"; it establishes hierarchy and guides users to important content.

Typography is "the art and technique of arranging type to make written language legible, readable and appealing"; it involves typeface, point size, line length, line spacing (leading), and letter spacing.

Gestalt principles explain grouping: "We group closer-together elements, separating them from those farther apart" (proximity), and matching visual characteristics read as one group (similarity) — the perceptual basis for proximity and repetition.

Analysis of 150+ user onboarding experiences identifies eight best practices: leverage social proof, avoid email verification walls, minimize unnecessary steps, make product tours action-focused, segment users, emphasize user goals, use helpful empty states, and send behavior-based emails.

Harvard Business Review analysis of Apple's organizational structure: a functional model where experts lead experts, enabling innovation at scale, as Apple grew from $7B to $260B in revenue under this leadership model.

Engineering deep-dive into how Prefixy built a scalable prefix search service to power autocomplete functionality, covering architecture and design decisions.
Wikipedia article on Hick's Law, which describes how the time to make a decision increases with the number and complexity of choices. Page was inaccessible (user-agent blocked).
